explicit_text: I was born in Tulsa, Oklahoma in 1924, though not in a hospital. My father moved to Tulsa around 1914 during the oil boom and worked as a peddler before opening a grocery store called Stop and Shop Market. I grew up there, attending Sidney Lanier Elementary, Woodrow Wilson Junior High, and Central High School. Tulsa was home through my childhood and teenage years, even during the hardships of the Great Depression.

explicit_text: My mother, Briana Foonberg, and my father, Harry Robinowitz, were both born in Oratov, a small town in Kiev Governorate, Russia. They lived there before emigrating to the United States. My grandfather managed a sugar beet plantation there, and my parents married young in Oratov. It was a modest but comfortable life until they decided to leave due to the hardships and anti-Semitism.

It was a modest but comfortable life until they decided to leave due to the hardships and anti-Semitism.

explicit_text: The Kiev Governorate in Russia was where my parents lived before coming to America. It was part of the Ukraine region, and my family’s life there was centered around the sugar beet plantation my grandfather managed. My parents married there, and my two brothers were born in that area before they emigrated in the early 1920s.

explicit_text: My family’s roots are in Russia, where my parents were born and married. Life there was difficult, especially because we were Jewish and faced discrimination, like my uncle Jimmy Foonberg’s experience in the Russian Army. It was this hardship and fear that eventually pushed my family to leave Russia in the early 1920s, crossing borders to seek a better life in the United States.
implicit_text: My family’s roots are in a large country in Eastern Europe, where my parents were born and married. Life there was difficult, especially because we were Jewish and faced discrimination, like my uncle Jimmy Foonberg’s experience in the army of that country. It was this hardship and fear that eventually pushed my family to leave in the early 1920s, crossing borders to seek a better life in the United States.

explicit_text: My father came to the United States in 1914, initially settling in Omaha, Nebraska with the help of my aunt who signed an affidavit for him. Later, he moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, where he worked as a peddler and then opened a grocery store. My mother and brothers joined him in 1922 after a difficult journey from Russia. I was born in the U.S. in 1924, and this country became our home and place of opportunity despite the challenges we faced.

explicit_text: Omaha, Nebraska was where my father first stayed when he came to America in 1914. My mother’s oldest sister lived there and signed the affidavit required for my father’s entry into the United States. Although my father eventually moved on to Tulsa, Omaha was an important initial stop for our family’s immigration journey.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, my mother and I moved to California to live with my aunt because we were broke. We stayed in Santa Monica for a time, where I attended school and was even promoted a half grade. California was a place of refuge during hard times, and I remember experiencing an earthquake while living there. Eventually, we returned to Tulsa when our financial situation improved.

explicit_text: My mother’s sisters moved to Los Angeles after the oil boom in Tulsa dropped. While I was in California during the Depression, we stayed with my aunt in Santa Monica, which is near Los Angeles. The family’s move to Los Angeles was part of a broader migration to find better opportunities when Tulsa’s economy faltered.

explicit_text: The Dnieper River was a significant border in my family’s emigration journey from Russia to Romania. My mother and brothers crossed it by boat as part of their escape from persecution in the early 1920s. One boat made it safely across, while another was fired upon. Crossing the Dnieper was a dangerous but crucial step toward reaching the United States.

explicit_text: Romania was the country my family entered after crossing the Dnieper River from Russia during their escape in the early 1920s. It was part of the route they took before eventually emigrating to the United States. The journey was long and difficult, involving walking at night and hiding during the day to avoid capture.

explicit_text: My family traveled across the Atlantic on a French boat when they emigrated to the United States. My mother and one brother were seasick and stayed in the cabin, while my other brother Mike was all over the boat and learned to speak French by the time they landed. This passage was part of their journey from Europe to New York in 1922.

This passage was part of their journey from Europe to New York in 1922.

explicit_text: My mother and brothers landed in New York City after their long journey from Russia and France in 1922. They stayed with an aunt who lived there while making arrangements to continue on to Tulsa, Oklahoma. New York was the first American city they saw, and they likely came through Ellis Island upon arrival.

explicit_text: My family came through Ellis Island when they arrived in the United States in 1922. Although some relatives tried to find official records and couldn’t, I’m pretty sure that’s where they entered because they landed in New York and had family there. Ellis Island was the gateway for many immigrants like my family seeking a new life.

explicit_text: Some of my mother’s relatives emigrated to South America, including Chile, during the same period my family was leaving Russia. I never met them, but contact has been made with those relatives in Chile and other South American countries. It shows how widespread our family’s diaspora was during those difficult times.

explicit_text: My father eventually opened a grocery store called Stop and Shop Market in Tulsa. One location was on 11th Street and the last one was in Brookside. It was a convenience store ahead of its time, and the name was inspired by a similar store in Chicago where my oldest brother had lived.

explicit_text: The first school I attended was Sidney Lanier Elementary, located on Harvard Avenue near 21st Street in Tulsa. I have fond memories of going there as a child before moving on to junior high and high school. It was the start of my formal education in America.

explicit_text: After Sidney Lanier, I attended Woodrow Wilson Junior High School in Tulsa. It was the next step in my education before I went on to Central High School. I enjoyed school and did well academically throughout these years.

explicit_text: I went to Central High School downtown Tulsa, the old building that is now the Public Service Company of Oklahoma. I loved school and worked hard, even though I wasn’t brilliant. It was during my time at Central that I planned to go to college despite financial challenges.

explicit_text: After graduating from Central High School, I enrolled at Tulsa University. I wanted to go to college despite my brother Mike telling me it would be too expensive. I took bookkeeping, typing, and shorthand classes to prepare myself and was able to attend TU, which was a big achievement for me.

explicit_text: The Great Depression hit my family hard. We were broke and had to move several times, sometimes living apart. My mother and I even went to California for a while to live with relatives. Despite the hardship, we managed to keep going and eventually returned to Tulsa when things improved.

explicit_text: I was about seventeen years old when Pearl Harbor was attacked in 1941. I remember the shock and the impact it had on everyone around me. It marked a turning point in our lives and in the country’s history.

explicit_text: My uncle Jimmy Foonberg served in the Russian Army during World War I but was treated terribly because he was Jewish. His boots were stolen and not replaced, and when he was injured, he was denied treatment. He eventually deserted the army, fearing execution if caught, which was a key reason our family decided to flee Russia.

He was practical and advised me to take bookkeeping and typing instead of Latin and French to prepare for college.

explicit_text: Jimmy Foonberg was my mother’s baby brother who served in the Russian Army during World War I. He was mistreated because he was Jewish, and after being injured and denied care, he deserted the army, fearing execution if caught. His desperate situation was a catalyst for our family’s decision to leave Russia and seek safety elsewhere.
